front: Kingdom: located to the south of, or centered on the mouth of, a namesake central African river, in present-day northern Angola
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Kingdom: lent its name to the Congo River and, much later, to the Republic of the Congo and Democratic Republic of the Congo
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Kingdom: founded, per tradition, by a "nami" named Lukeni lua Nimi, with its capital at the town of Mbanza
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Ruler: this Kongolese mani/mwene became the kingdom's first Christian ruler after contact with Portuguese explorer Diogo Cao, taking a Portuguese baptismal name in 1491
work: Joao I (Nzinga a Nkuwu) and the Conversion to Christianity

front: Ruler: rebuilt the kingdom's capital, Mbanza, in stone and renamed it Sao Salvador
work: Joao I (Nzinga a Nkuwu) and the Conversion to Christianity

front: Ruler: reverted to traditional beliefs, despite his earlier conversion to Christianity, after his burning of idols angered his people
work: Joao I (Nzinga a Nkuwu) and the Conversion to Christianity

front: Ruler: won a civil war against his half-brother after an apparition of Saint James the Greater (leading five horsemen) caused his brother's army to flee; the image became the kingdom's coat of arms until 1859
work: Afonso I and the Christianization of Kongo

front: Ruler: this king's son Henrique was appointed bishop of Utica in 1518 by Pope Leo X, likely becoming the first Black African bishop, and died en route to the Council of Trent
work: Afonso I and the Christianization of Kongo

front: Ruler: fully established Catholicism as the kingdom's state religion, taking a regnal name from Portugal's Aviz dynasty
work: Afonso I and the Christianization of Kongo

front: Ruler: wrote letters (including one in 1539) to Portuguese kings Manuel I and Joao III protesting the kidnapping and enslavement of his nephews
work: Afonso I and the Christianization of Kongo

front: Ruler: despite protesting the slave trade to Portugal, organized his own kingdom's participation in it to prevent illegal enslavement of his subjects
work: Afonso I and the Christianization of Kongo

front: Ruler: started a celebration crowning the leaders of religious brotherhoods as king, which later inspired the Dutch-American holiday Pinkster
work: Afonso I and the Christianization of Kongo

front: Ruler: ruled Nsundi province before coming to the throne
work: Afonso I and the Christianization of Kongo

front: Ruler: began his reign by battling Jaga invaders, then allowed the Portuguese to found the outpost of Luanda in exchange for military assistance
work: Portuguese Relations, Trade, and Rival Kingdoms

front: Event: this kingdom warred with its neighbor Ndongo (formally its vassal), ruled by Queen Nzinga, who later allied with the Dutch against Portugal
work: Portuguese Relations, Trade, and Rival Kingdoms

front: Event: this kingdom's ambassador Antonio Manuel ne Vunda was detained by Portuguese officials for three years, preventing him from reporting clerical abuses to Pope Paul V
work: Portuguese Relations, Trade, and Rival Kingdoms

front: Event: sold enslaved people at markets around the Malebo Pool to traders from Sao Tome, and was forced to send captives to work sugar plantations on Sao Tome and Principe
work: Portuguese Relations, Trade, and Rival Kingdoms

front: Kingdom: this state was the birthplace of the Spanish conquistador-explorer Juan Garrido
work: Portuguese Relations, Trade, and Rival Kingdoms

front: Event: in 1665, King Antonio I was defeated and killed at this engagement; the Silva family then dominated the throne, sparking a 50-year civil war between the Kimpanzu and Kinlaza houses
work: Civil War, the Battle of Mbwila, and Antonianism

front: Figure: this Kongolese noblewoman claimed to be possessed by Saint Anthony of Padua and led a syncretic religious movement during Kongo's civil war
work: Civil War, the Battle of Mbwila, and Antonianism

front: Event: this movement taught that Jesus was born in Kongo and was a Black man, and its followers destroyed crosses and used their own version of the Salve Regina prayer
work: Civil War, the Battle of Mbwila, and Antonianism

front: Event: followers of this movement reoccupied the abandoned Kongo capital of Sao Salvador in an attempt to reunify the kingdom during its civil war
work: Civil War, the Battle of Mbwila, and Antonianism

front: Ruler: this king's ascension ended Kongo's civil war; he fought the Antonian movement and had Kimpa Vita (Dona Beatriz) burned at the stake as a heretic in 1706
work: Civil War, the Battle of Mbwila, and Antonianism

front: Event: this rebellious vassal state of Kongo, ruled by its own counts, defeated the Portuguese at the Battle of Kitombo before later being reunified with the kingdom
work: Civil War, the Battle of Mbwila, and Antonianism

front: Event: a civil war arose in Kongo when this pretender attempted to usurp the throne
work: Civil War, the Battle of Mbwila, and Antonianism

front: Institution: this kingdom's royal council, whose name translated to "the top of the top," was divided into bureaucrats, electors, and matrons
work: Society, Religion, and the Bakongo Cultural Legacy

front: Concept: this kingdom was administered through towns called mbanzas
work: Society, Religion, and the Bakongo Cultural Legacy

front: Concept: currency included nzimbu shells and cundis, high-quality raffia cloths that were also the kingdom's main export
work: Society, Religion, and the Bakongo Cultural Legacy

front: Concept: a tax was assessed on the kingdom's subjects whenever the monarch's beret fell off his head
work: Society, Religion, and the Bakongo Cultural Legacy

front: Concept: nail-studded "power figures" created by the Bakongo people, likely the inspiration for voodoo dolls
work: Society, Religion, and the Bakongo Cultural Legacy

front: Concept: this four-pointed Bakongo cosmogram, shaped like a cross and depicting the four moments of the sun, eased the adoption of Christian cross iconography
work: Society, Religion, and the Bakongo Cultural Legacy

front: Concept: through the Atlantic slave trade, Kongo religious concepts and iconography spread to shape traditions such as Haitian Vodou, Cuban Palo Monte, and Jamaican Myal
work: Society, Religion, and the Bakongo Cultural Legacy

front: Figure: this nobleman born in the Kingdom of Kongo later led the Quilombo dos Palmares maroon settlement in Brazil
work: Society, Religion, and the Bakongo Cultural Legacy

front: Ruler: this king ceded the Kingdom of Kongo to Portugal, leading to its eventual integration into colonial Angola
work: Decline and Legacy

front: Figure: this 20th-century Congolese politician attempted to restore the Kingdom of Kongo
work: Decline and Legacy
